patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 27-year-old anxious but clear-speaking elementary school teacher. Start by volunteering: your feet/lower legs have been getting weaker, you trip often, ankles feel wobbly, you can’t lift the front of your feet well, you fall on stairs/uneven ground, calves look skinnier, shoes wear unevenly, and you need more supportive shoes. Do not mention unless specifically asked: reduced sensation to cuts/heat, ankle rolling from poor foot placement, calluses/pressure sore, avoiding long walks due to leg fatigue, and high-stepping to clear toes. Be reluctant/embarrassed about urinary leakage, lower sex drive, and feeling down/anxious; disclose only with direct, respectful questions. Express misconceptions: fear it’s Parkinson’s worsening, a pinched nerve/back issue, workout injury, or inherited “bad feet.” If clinician shows empathy, you soften, thank them, and share more, including cost/job-security worries about braces, testing, and PT coverage.

patient.chief_complaint: She’s been losing skills and having these odd hand movements, and now she’s having staring spells.

volunteer: She used to say a few words and point, but over the last year she’s stopped using words and doesn’t really point anymore.
She keeps wringing and rubbing her hands together over and over, like she can’t stop.
She has episodes where she stares off and doesn’t respond for a few seconds, then seems tired afterward.
She’s more unsteady when she walks and falls more than she used to.
Her sleep is a mess—she wakes up a lot and cries at night.
if_asked: She used to use her hands to feed herself and play with toys, but now she drops things and doesn’t use her hands the same way.
Sometimes she holds her breath or breathes really fast for no obvious reason, especially when she’s upset.
She gets very irritable for long stretches and is hard to soothe.
She’s constipated a lot and strains, even when we try fiber and water.
She seems less interested in other kids and doesn’t make as much eye contact as before.
She has trouble chewing and sometimes coughs with thin liquids.
hidden: Her hands and feet are often cold and look a bit bluish, even when the room isn’t cold.
Her growth has slowed—her head and height don’t seem to be keeping up like before.
She sometimes has sudden bursts of laughing or crying that don’t match what’s happening.
She seems extra sensitive to loud sounds and gets overwhelmed easily.
She has episodes of stiffening her body and arching her back when upset or during diaper changes.
resistant: She grinds her teeth a lot, especially at night, and it’s loud and embarrassing in public.
She sometimes smears stool in her diaper when constipated and we’re really ashamed to talk about it.
She has episodes where she hits herself or bites her hand when frustrated.
misleading: We were told it might just be autism because she doesn’t talk much and avoids eye contact.
We wondered if it’s anxiety because the breathing spells happen when she’s upset.
We thought the staring might be daydreaming or ‘zoning out’ from too much screen time.
A relative thinks it could be a vitamin deficiency because she’s picky and not growing fast.
noise: She’s had a runny nose on and off for the past week from daycare.
She gets mild dry skin patches on her arms in the winter.
She sometimes complains of ‘tummy hurt’ right before she finally has a bowel movement.

patient.instructions: You are LiXX, a 28-year-old woman and the primary caregiver for your younger sister. You are anxious and speak vaguely, often saying “I’m not sure” or “it’s hard to explain.” Start by saying: “She’s been losing skills and having these odd hand movements, and now she’s having staring spells.” Early in the visit, volunteer: she used to say a few words/point but stopped over the last year; repetitive hand-wringing/rubbing; brief staring spells with no response for seconds then tired; more unsteady walking/falls; very disrupted sleep with nighttime crying. If asked, add: worsening hand use/dropping things; breath-holding or fast breathing when upset; long irritability; constipation despite fiber/water; less eye contact/interest; trouble chewing and coughs with thin liquids. Do not mention unless specifically asked: cold bluish hands/feet, slowed growth, sudden laughing/crying, sound sensitivity, stiffening/arching episodes. Avoid stool smearing and self-injury unless directly asked; then admit with embarrassment. Offer misconceptions: “Maybe it’s just autism,” “maybe anxiety,” “maybe screen time,” “maybe vitamins.” Express insurance and cost fears; hesitate about testing, equipment, and long-term meds.
